attributes.stem: A four-year-old boy presents to the Emergency Department with a two-day history of diarrhoea. His diarrhoea is watery, initially with a stool frequency of three to four times per day but the frequency has increased to almost one to two times per hour in the last 24 hours. He has mild, generalised abdominal pain. He has not vomited but feels nauseated and refuses to drink or eat. He feels dizzy when standing up and his urine output is decreased. In the last few hours he has become less active than usual. On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 90/min, blood pressure 90/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. He looks pale with sunken eyes. He is irritable. Capillary refill time is greater than 2 seconds. Oral mucous membranes are dry and skin turgor is mildly decreased.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate immediate management?
attributes.explanation:
The most appropriate immediate management is to correct the dehydration. Intravenous rehydration is the initial best option in this child, though generally oral rehydration is preferred in gastroenteritis with mild to moderate dehydration. This child has signs of severe dehydration including irritability, sunken eyes, prolonged capillary refill, and dry mucous membranes.

Nasogastric rehydration is an alternative to intravenous fluids in some children with severe dehydration who refuse to drink. However, with the irritability seen in this child, insertion of the nasogastric tube and its retention may be fairly traumatic, and could precipitate vomiting and possibly pulmonary aspiration.

Ondansetron is an anti-emetic that can be used in children with gastroenteritis and vomiting. It may reduce the need for intravenous fluids and hospitalization. However, this child has not been vomiting, so ondansetron is not the most appropriate initial management.

Oral rehydration solution is the first-line treatment for mild to moderate dehydration in children with gastroenteritis. However, in this case, the child is severely dehydrated and refusing to drink, making oral rehydration impractical and potentially insufficient for rapid correction of the fluid deficit.

Zinc supplementation reduces the duration and severity of diarrhoea in children, especially in developing countries where zinc deficiency is common. However, in this Australian child, intravenous rehydration to correct severe dehydration is the most urgent priority, and should be instituted before considering zinc supplementation.

In most cases of acute gastroenteritis in children, the cause is viral and self-limiting. Antibiotics are not routinely indicated. In fact, antibiotic therapy should be avoided in cases suspected to be caused by Shiga-toxin-producing Escherichia coli (such as E. coli O157:H7), as it may increase the risk of haemolytic uraemic syndrome. The priority in management is always to address dehydration, which can be life-threatening if severe.
learningPoints: Most infants and children with gastroenteritis can be rehydrated orally.
Antibiotics are not indicated in most children with acute gastroenteritis.

Antibiotic therapy should be avoided in gastroenteritis caused by Escherichia coli O157:H7 (Shiga-toxin-producing E. coli). Antibiotic therapy in this situation increases the risk of haemolytic uraemic syndrome.
references: Webb A, Starr M. Acute gastroenteritis in children. Aust Fam Physician. 2005 Apr;34(4):227-31.

King CK, Glass R, Bresee JS, Duggan C. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Managing acute gastroenteritis among children: oral rehydration, maintenance, and nutritional therapy. MMWR Recomm Rep. 2003 Nov 21;52(RR-16):1-16.
Bruzzese E, Giannattasio A, Guarino A. Antibiotic treatment of acute gastroenteritis in children. F1000Res. 2018;7:193. Published 2018 Feb 15. doi:10.12688/f1000research.12328.1
Farquhar D. E. coli, antibiotics and hemolytic-uremic syndrome in children. CMAJ. 2000;163(4):438.
